risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "An independent researcher made the allocation schedule with a computerized random number generator and the investigators had no knowledge of the schedule." Comment: This explicitly describes a proper random method (computerized random number generator) and also indicates independent preparation of the schedule, so the allocation sequence is truly random.

_evidence.method_quote: An independent researcher made the allocation schedule with a computerized random number generator and the investigators had no knowledge of the schedule.
_evidence.method_summary: An independent researcher created the allocation schedule using a computerized random number generator, with stratification and block randomization.
context_clues: Randomization took place at an individual level
Block randomization was used with random blocks containing 4, 6, or 8 allocations

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "An independent researcher made the allocation schedule with a computerized random number generator and the investigators had no knowledge of the schedule." Comment: This explicitly states that an independent researcher created the allocation schedule and the investigators/recruiters did not know it, indicating a concealment process that prevented foreknowledge of the next assignment.

_evidence.concealment_quote: An independent researcher made the allocation schedule with a computerized random number generator and the investigators had no knowledge of the schedule.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The participants were informed about randomization outcome via email." "Participants randomized to the CAU group received an email with the randomization outcome only..." "This study was a randomized controlled trial with 2 arms: a Web-based guided self-help intervention ... and a CAU group." Comment: Participants were not blinded and personnel delivering the web-based intervention were necessarily aware of allocation; this knowledge could plausibly influence the reported outcomes, which were largely subjective/self-reported (CES-D, MBI, work performance, HADS).

_evidence.blinding_quote: "This study was a randomized controlled trial with 2 arms: a Web-based guided self-help intervention (called Happy@Work) and a CAU group." "The participants were informed about randomization outcome via email." "Participants randomized to the CAU group received an email with the randomization outcome only and were advised to consult their (occupational) physician or a psychologist if they wanted treatment for their depressive symptoms."

risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The outcome assessors were the participants themselves for most outcomes, and the study used multiple patient-reported measures: CES-D depressive symptoms, MBI exhaustion/cynicism/reduced professional efficacy, absenteeism, work performance, HADS anxiety, and health care utilization. The article states these were self-report questionnaires (e.g., "The CES-D consists of 20 items"; "This self-report questionnaire contains 15 items"; "The participant was asked to report"; "We used ... to measure work performance"; "The HADS ... was used"; "The TiC-P is a self-report questionnaire"). Participants were informed of randomization outcome via email, so self-reported outcomes were not blinded. Because subjective/patient-reported outcomes are present and the patient is the assessor, knowledge of allocation could bias measurement, so the domain is High risk despite the separate clinical interview outcome being structured.

Quote: "All analyses were performed according to the intention-to-treat (ITT) principle. Missing data were handled by multiple imputation via data augmentation." Comment: The paper reports overall attrition at the analysed posttreatment time point as 26% (171/231 retained), with balanced randomised groups at baseline and no evidence that missingness was due to outcome-related reasons; primary analyses used ITT with multiple imputation, which reduces concern that incomplete outcome data biased the results.

per_arm_flow.reasons: A total of 116 participants were randomized to the intervention group. At posttreatment, 26% attrition for the study sample overall; intervention adherence: "A total of 29 of 116 participants dropped out of the intervention at their own request or because of prolonged inactivity on the website. The other participants were not able to complete more lessons within the time limit of 7 weeks."

A total of 115 participants were randomized to the CAU group. The paper states that "Participants in the CAU group completed the posttreatment assessment ... and the 6-month follow-up assessment ... more often."

_evidence.missing_data_method: ITT; multiple imputation via data augmentation
_evidence.notes: Attrition numbers are reported overall for the study sample at posttreatment, 6 months, and 12 months; no arm-specific loss counts are given. The per-protocol analysis compared treatment completers (completed ≥3 lessons) to CAU, but the primary analyses were ITT with multiple imputation.
